के साथ तालमेल के आधार पर िकया जाता है।

SHRI KODIKUNNIL SURESH: Hon. Speaker Sir, the hon. Minister has given the
reply about coverage of national highways in the country. But in Kerala, the
coverage of national highways is very nominal. There is a total of 12 national
highways in Kerala and the total coverage is 1858 kilometres. This figure is very
low as compared to other States. There are a number of proposals for declaring
new highways in Kerala. I would like to know from the hon. Minister about the
proposals submitted by the Government of Kerala and the MPs of Kerala to
upgrade the national highways in the State.
27.03.2025 21
SHRI NITIN JAIRAM GADKARI: Hon. Speaker Sir, in Kerala, the situation is totally
different. The cost of construction with land acquisition comes to Rs.100 crore per
kilometre and the land acquisition cost is Rs.49 crore to Rs.50 crore per kilometre.
Previously, it was promised by the hon. Chief Minister that they would contribute
50 per cent cost of the land acquisition and the State Government of Kerala would
contribute Rs.5,000 crore. But after that, it seemed to be very difficult because the
Budget of the State Government of Kerala is very small. I called the hon. Chief
Minister and we had a discussion. After that, we compromised the problem. We
have already found a solution. In case of land acquisition, if the land is there, the
State Government will give that land to NHAI free of cost. Secondly, the royalty on
aggregate sand and stone will be free; and thirdly, in case of the cost of
construction, wherever we need cement and steel, there is 18 per cent GST out of
which nine per cent GST is from the State Government. I requested them to give
their contribution in this. The Kerala Government has already accepted that and
now we are in a position to consider the project.
Now the problem is that we are constructing the national highway from
Trivendrum to Kanniyakumari and it is a big four-lane project. But at the same time,
it is very difficult in Kerala because as far as the urban laws are concerned, the
roadside development is everywhere. When any MP from Kerala comes to me, he
asks me for underpass. Now I am in a position that I am not making the road or the
underpass. Today, I have a meeting with all the Members of Parliament. Hon.
Members from Kerala are here. Everyone is demanding underpass. It is quite
difficult. Still, in this situation, in view of the federal character of the country, it is the
27.03.2025 22
duty of the Central Government not to discriminate against Kerala. That is the
reason that for Kerala, we have given Rs.50,000 crore additionally for the work.
As far as the requirement under the criteria for declaring a road as national
highway is concerned, it is on the basis of port connectivity, industrial clusters,
religious clusters etc. Yesterday, I had a meeting with the Members of Parliament.
We have found a solution for road connecting famous religious places in Kerala.
We are very positive and we will try to resolve the issue. Whenever we need and if
a road is coming within the criteria of being declared a national highway, we will
consider it and we will try to totally support the development of road infrastructure
in Kerala.

(cid:174)ी बजरगं मनोहर सोनवणे : माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) जी, आज एक मह(cid:194)वपूण(cid:170) िवषय पर चचा(cid:170) हो रही ह ै ।
‘(cid:214)व(cid:184)छ भारत िमशन-(cid:250)ामीण’ के अंतग(cid:170)त सीएससी और आईएचएचएल के िनमा(cid:170)ण हेतु महारा(cid:213)(cid:367) के
सामदु ाियक (cid:214)व(cid:184)छता प(cid:229)रसर म (cid:164) सीएससी और (cid:211)यि(cid:265)गत घरेलू शौचालय(cid:332) म (cid:164) आईएचएचएल के िनमा(cid:170)ण
का सार दर साल िववरण (cid:179)या ह?ै उसक(cid:236) तुलना रा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय औसत से कै से क(cid:236) जाती है? महारा(cid:213)(cid:367) के िकन
िजल(cid:332) म(cid:164) सीएससी और आईएचएचएल िनमा(cid:170)ण क(cid:236) दर सबसे अिधक और सबस ेकम है? दशे के मुकाबले
उसक(cid:236) तुलना कै से होती है और उसका संभव कारण (cid:179)या है?
(cid:174)ी सी. आर. पािटल : माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) जी, (cid:250)ामीण िव(cid:214)तार म(cid:164) (cid:214)व(cid:184)छता म(cid:164) ओडीएफ क(cid:236) क(cid:208)पना को
साकार करने के िलए माननीय (cid:255)धानमं(cid:253)ी (cid:174)ी नर(cid:198)े (cid:254) मोदी साहब ने एक अिभयान शु(cid:322) िकया था । पहले
पांच साल म(cid:164) करीब 10 करोड़ टॉयलेट्स क(cid:236) (cid:211)यव(cid:214)था क(cid:236) गई है । उसका प(cid:229)रणाम यह ह(cid:242)आ िक करीब
60 करोड़ लोग(cid:332) ने अपनी आदत(cid:164) बदली ह(cid:167) । उ(cid:198)ह(cid:332)ने खलु े म(cid:164) शौच ि(cid:248)या करने क(cid:236) बजाय वे टॉयलेट
(ओडीएफ) का उपयोग करने लगे ह(cid:167) । उसके बह(cid:242)त अ(cid:184)छे प(cid:229)रणाम भी िमले है ।

म(cid:253)ं ालय ने इस योजना को नया (cid:322)प िदया है और चार घटक(cid:332) अथा(cid:170)त लाभाथ(cid:234) आधा(cid:229)रत िनमा(cid:170)ण
(बीएलसी), साझेदारी म(cid:164) िकफायती आवास (एएचपी), िकफायती िकराया आवास (एआरएच) और (cid:202)याज
सि(cid:202)सडी योजना (आईएसएस) के मा(cid:197)यम से िकफायती लागत पर 1 करोड़ अित(cid:229)र(cid:265) पा(cid:253) लाभािथ(cid:170)य(cid:332)

27.03.2025 39
THE MINISTER OF CIVIL AVIATION (SHRI KINJARAPU RAMMOHAN NAIDU):
(a) to (f): A Statement is laid on the Table of the House.
STATEMENT
(a): Ministry of Civil Aviation launched the Regional Connectivity Scheme - UDAN
(Ude Desh ka Aam Nagrik) on 21-10-2016 to stimulate regional air connectivity
from unserved and underserved airports for the expansion of air services in small
cities and remote areas in the country by making air travel affordable to the masses.
More than 149 lakhs domestic passengers have travelled through RCS-UDAN in
more than 2.97 lakh RCS flights.
(b) to (f): The Scheme provides financial incentives to airline operators on regional
routes that include: Concessions by the Central Government, State Governments/
Union Territories and airport operators to reduce airline operation costs and also
the Viability Gap Funding (VGF) to bridge the gap between operational costs and
expected revenues on such routes so as to make air travel affordable under the
scheme.
Airlines are required to commit 50% of the seats as RCS seats (limited to 40
seats) on RCS flights. The airfare on the RCS seats, on which VGF is provided to
the Selected Airline Operators (SAOs) for a period of 3-years, is capped at a
subsidised rate and indexed every quarter. Remaining seats are sold by the airlines
as non-RCS seats on commercial fares.
The Government does not regulate the commercial fares set by airlines -
Indian or foreign. The fares on any route are dependent, inter-alia, on seasonality,
holidays and festivals, cost of Aviation Turbine Fuel, competition and other similar
27.03.2025 40
factors. Airline pricing runs in multiple levels {buckets or RBDs} which are in line
with the practice being followed globally and due to dynamic fare pricing, the tickets
bought in advance are much cheaper than those purchased near the travel date.
Under the provisions of Rule 135(1) of the Aircraft Rules, 1937, the airlines are free
to fix tariffs, having regard to all relevant factors, including cost of operation,
characteristics of services, generally prevailing tariff etc. DGCA's Tariff Monitoring
Unit (TMU) ensures that the fares being charged by the airlines are as per the tariff
established by the airlines.

करता ह(cid:243) ँ िक, वे ऊजा(cid:170)वान म(cid:253)ं ी ह(cid:167), व े सि(cid:248)य होकर काम कर रहे ह(cid:167), तो अव(cid:212)य ही कुछ बदलाव लेकर
आएगं े ।

27.03.2025 41
directly related to the airfares and on multiple times also, we have discussed about
this and I would also like to highlight that the airfare system presently is a dynamic
fare system. So, based on that, we want the airlines to compete with each other
and this is an international procedure that is followed by all the countries. They
have kept a dynamic airfare system which our country is also following. So, we do
not fix the airfares. But we let the airlines to decide based on the demand and
wherever we see that the airfares are rising or there is an increase in the airfare, it
is because of the higher demand in that sector or in that route.
And specifically, if you ask about the online booking, there are the online
ticketing agencies which charge additional fee for the service that they do. But
whereas the airlines sell the tickets directly with which they have set the base price
and for this, the airlines have their own websites, they have their own selling
agencies and then, there are online ticketing agencies. … (Interruptions) He is
talking about the online ticketing agencies – purchasing it online. It is not like that.
It has to be based on the demand. That is how, the airlines are deciding right now.

SHRI KINJARAPU RAMMOHAN NAIDU: Sir, the rules have originated at that time
but we continuously change according to the feedback and whenever there is
necessity, it keeps getting updated. When it is regarding the airfare, it has been
continuously come under discussion also and from the Ministry, we have been
committed to create an affordable system in the country. That is our commitment
to the nation also and that is how, we have started the UDAN scheme. The whole
purpose of the UDAN Scheme was to create affordable network in the country. …
(Interruptions) Please listen to me, please, listen to me. Let me explain the
situation.
Sir, there is a Tariff Monitoring Unit in the DGCA which looks after airfare
prices, and there is a tariff sheet that airlines have to provide to the DGCA before
the start of every season. Previously, airlines had the provision to change fares at
any time, but after thorough verification, we have implemented a system requiring
them to submit the tariff sheet at the beginning of each month, prohibiting any
changes for the entire month. This is one change we have introduced…
(Interruptions)
Regarding airfares, 45 per cent is attributed to airfare fuel, with ATF
accounting for this significant portion. Some States charge up to 29 per cent VAT
on ATF, with Tamil Nadu having the highest ATF in the country… (Interruptions) I
have written to all the Chief Ministers requesting a reduction in these charges.
Currently, 15 States have reduced it below five per cent, while ten States have kept
it below five per cent. But some States are still charging higher rates…
(Interruptions)
27.03.2025 43
I urge the hon. Members representing those States to find out their ATF
charges and work towards lowering them, as 45 per cent of airfare prices stem from
ATF and VAT… (Interruptions)
SHRI A. RAJA: Sir, I patiently heard the hon. Minister. There is a dynamic system,
which is being followed globally.
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) : (cid:174)ी ए. राजा जी, आपने धैय(cid:170) रखा है, इसिलए म(cid:167)ने आपको बोलने का मौका िदया
ह ै ।
मारन जी धैय(cid:170) नह(cid:233) रखते ह(cid:167), इसिलए इनको बोलने के िलए मौका नह(cid:233) िमलेगा ।
SHRI A. RAJA: But I can suggest another approach. Injecting competition into the
sector will naturally lead to lower tariffs. We acknowledge the difficulties we face.
But similar to the telecom sector, increasing the number of operators will
result in reduced tariffs. This has occurred in our country. What has prevented the
Ministry from fostering more competition in the sector? Is there any honest attempt
made by the Government?
SHRI KINJARAPU RAMMOHAN NAIDU: The market is entirely open, and we are
not restricting any airline from operating in the country. Our constant objective is to
encourage more airlines to enter the market, and with the initiation of the UDAAN
scheme, we have seen smaller airlines coming into picture.
Now, operators capable of handling 9-seater, 18-seater, and 40-seater
aircraft are present, which was not the case before. The establishment of airports
in smaller sectors, Tier 2 cities and Tier 3 cities has allowed many smaller airline
operators with just two or three planes to enter the market.
I agree that we need more competition. This is simple economics: more
27.03.2025 44
competition and more flights will undoubtedly lead to lower tariffs. Under the
leadership of our hon. Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi ji, aviation sector has
expanded significantly over the last ten years with the number of airports increasing
from 74 to 159… (Interruptions)
Many MPs are also benefiting from this, as they now have airports close to
their constituencies. Our focus now must be on improving connectivity. There are
only a limited number of planes in the country. In 2014, we had around 340 planes,
and now we have increased that number to at least 840. Currently, there are about
1700 pending orders for planes. Why would airlines order 1700 planes if there was
no demand?
The growth of aviation is remarkable, with passenger capacity increasing by
more than ten per cent annually, a feat unmatched by any other country at this
stage. The most crucial aspect is to increase the airline fleet in the country, and we
are doing our utmost to facilitate this. We are making it easier for those wishing to
operate in this space to obtain the necessary permissions to operate their planes.
One important point I would like to elaborate on, as it frequently arises in
discussions, is our efforts to increase the number of planes. The leasing industry
has evolved, with airlines preferring to lease planes rather than purchase them, as
this reduces their cost burden. Airlines are leasing from international destinations
such as Ireland, Dubai, and Singapore. However, Narendra Modi ji has proposed
establishing a leasing industry within our country.
We are proud to announce that GIFT City is creating an ecosystem where
we have already leased out more than 200 planes, with 200 entities involved.
27.03.2025 45
Additionally, we are introducing a law to ratify the Cape Town Convention, which
will reduce leasing costs by eight to ten per cent. When it becomes more easier for
these airlines to lease these airplanes, then, definitely, reducing leasing costs will
ultimately benefit passengers by lowering airfares. So, we are taking a multiple
approach. It is not just capping of the airfares, we are bringing in a system where
from all sides, whatever issues are there, we are going to tackle them.

(cid:255)(cid:307) सं(cid:180)या 386, (cid:174)ी कंुद(cid:321)ु रघुवीर ।
ODOP FOR NALGONDA IN TELANGANA
*386. SHRI KUNDURU RAGHUVEER:
Will the Minister of FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware that sweet oranges identified as the One
District One Product (ODOP) for Nalgonda district in Telangana face commercial
limitations due to bitterness-causing compounds like limonin and naringin and if
so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Government has any plans to collaborate with Central Food
Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysuru and Indian Institute of Food
Processing Technology (IIFPT), Thanjavur to develop technology for reducing
limonin content to make sweet orange juice commercially viable;
27.03.2025 53
(c) if so, the timeline for developing and making such technology accessible to the
farmers to enhance the market demand;
(d) whether the Government is contemplating to revise Nalgonda’s ODOP and
replace sweet oranges with crops like cotton or paddy which are better suited to
the region’s climate and market conditions and if so, the details thereof;
(e) the criteria for selecting ODOPs and whether any mechanism exists to revise
them based on farmers' feedback, if so, the details thereof; and
(f) the steps taken/being taken by the Government to ensure that farmers benefit
from ODOP initiatives or receive support for alternative crops if a revision is
made?
THE MINISTER OF FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES (SHRI CHIRAG
PASWAN):
(a) to (f): A Statement is laid on the Table of the House.
STATEMENT
(a) to (f): Sweet oranges have been identified as the One District One Product
(ODOP) for Nalgonda District in Telangana based on the recommendations of the
State Government of Telangana. Sweet orange was chosen as the ODOP (One
District One Product) for Nalgonda District by the State Government due to its
suitability for cultivation, high productivity and predominance of crop in the region.
Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI) has been extending financial
assistance to undertake demand driven Research and Development (R&D) work
for the benefit food processing industry in terms of product and process
27.03.2025 54
development, efficient technologies, improved packaging, value addition etc. with
commercial value along with standardization of various factors.
However, MoFPI has not received R&D proposal neither from State
Government of Telangana nor from any of the Research Institutions including
Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysuru and Indian
Institute of Food Processing Technology (now renamed as NIFTEM), Thanjavur for
supporting development of technology for reducing bitterness causing compounds
to make sweet orange juice commercially viable.
In order to change the ODOP for Nalgonda, the State Government has to
submit a proposal to MoFPI. However, no such proposal has been received from
the State Government of Telangana.
The organizations such as Central and State PSUs/ Joint Ventures/ Farmer
Producers Organization (FPOs)/ NGOs/ Cooperatives/ SHG’s/ Pvt. Ltd.
Companies/ Individuals proprietorship firms etc. including individual farmers are
eligible for availing financial support/incentive under the PMFME scheme subject
to compliance of the terms and conditions prescribed in the scheme guidelines.
The details of support available under PMFME Scheme for both ODOP and non-
ODOP are given in the enclosed Annexure.
ANNEXURE
The following support is provided under PMFME Scheme in the country for
all the products including ODOP products:
(i). Support to Individual / Group Category Micro Enterprises: Credit-linked capital
subsidy @35% of the eligible project cost, maximum ceiling Rs.10 lakh per unit;
27.03.2025 55
(ii). Support to SHGs for seed capital: Seed capital @ Rs. 40,000/- per member of
SHG engaged in food processing for working capital and purchase of small tools
subject to maximum of Rs. 4 lakhs per SHG Federation.
(iii). Support for Common Infrastructure: Credit linked capital subsidy @35%
subject to maximum of Rs. 3 crores to support FPOs, SHGs, Cooperatives and any
Government agency for setting up of common infrastructure.
(iv). Branding and Marketing Support: Grant upto 50% for Branding and Marketing
to groups of FPOs/ SHGs/ Cooperatives or an SPV of micro food processing
enterprises.
(v). Capacity Building: The scheme envisages training for Entrepreneurship
Development Program (EDP) to meet the requirement of food processing industry
and product specific skilling.
SHRI KUNDURU RAGHUVEER: Thank you, Speaker Sir.
Sweet oranges have been identified for the Nalgonda district as ‘One District,
One Product’ (ODOP), but there is a commercial limitation for it because of high
limonin content in it. Has the Government got any plans to collaborate with the
Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI) or if any processing
technology can be developed to make it commercially viable?
SHRI CHIRAG PASWAN: Sir, this was the question that was asked by the hon.
Member and reply has been given in detail.
As we all know that in the federal structure of our country, agriculture is a
State subject, and it is not related to the processing but it is specifically related to
the production quality which brings in these commercial limitations. Having said
27.03.2025 56
that, ODOP is a scheme that has been floated by the Central Government in which
the product is referred by the State Government. So, the State Government’s
recommended product in a particular district is being selected as ODOP. If the
State Government recommends any changes, then that would definitely be taken
into consideration.
Having said this, there are a couple of schemes that have been floated by
the Central Government. I would want to bring this on record, through the hon.
Member and through you, so that this can be conveyed to the State Government
as well. There are multiple schemes that have been floated by the Central
Government specifically for the micro enterprises. There is a scheme of PM FME
in which the Central Government has released an amount of Rs. 127.74 crore in
which a matching share from the State has to be released, which is equivalent to
Rs. 61.46 crore. It means a total of Rs. 189.20 crore is what is there with the State
Government in which they can suggest processing and they can suggest how to
improve the quality of the production.
I just want to bring this on record that unfortunately only Rs. 88.87 crore have
been utilized by the State Government as of now. If they really utilize the complete
amount, then we can always work on the R&D of the produce and also see how we
can increase the commercial expansion of the product as well.
Thank you, Sir.
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177): (cid:174)ी िजया उर रहमान – उपि(cid:214)थत नह(cid:233) ।
(cid:174)ी छोटेलाल – उपि(cid:214)थत नह(cid:233) ।
27.03.2025 57
FOOD WASTAGE AND POST-HARVEST LOSSES

*387. SHRI ZIA UR REHMAN:
SHRI CHHOTELAL:
Will the Minister of FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has taken cognizance of the fact that food wastage and
post-harvest losses have increased to a significant percentage of total agricultural
produce every year and if so, the details thereof;
(b) the details of the initiatives proposed by the Government to reduce food wastage
and improve food processing infrastructure in the country; and
(c) whether the Government realizes that a robust food processing sector is essential
for enhancing farmers’ income and promoting exports and if so, the details of the
steps taken/proposed to be taken in this regard?
THE MINISTER OF FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES (SHRI CHIRAG
PASWAN): Sir, the Statement is laid on the Table of the House.
STATEMENT
(a): As per studies conducted by Indian Council for Agricultural Research – Central
Institute of Post-Harvest Engineering and Technology (ICAR-CIPHET), 2015 and
NABARD Consultancy Service Pvt. Ltd (NABCONS), 2022, the estimated
percentage of Harvest and Post-Harvest loss of various agricultural produce in
India is as follows:
 Since Shri Zia Ur Rehman and Chhotelal were not present, Hon’ble speaker asked the Hon’ble Minister to lay the reply.
27.03.2025 58
Crops/ Commodities Estimated Percentage Loss
As per ICAR-CIPHET Study As per NABCONS study
(2015) (2022)
Cereals 4.65 - 5.99 3.89-5.92
Pulses 6.36 - 8.41 5.65-6.74
Oil Seeds 3.08 - 9.96 2.87-7.51
Fruits 6.70-15.88 6.02-15.05
Vegetables 4.58-12.44 4.87-11.61
Plantation Crops and Spices 1.18-7.89 1.29-7.33
Milk 0.92 0.87
Fisheries (Inland) 5.23 4.86
Fisheries (Marine) 10.52 8.76
Meat 2.71 2.34
Poultry 6.74 5.63
Egg 7.19 6.03
(b) and (c): The Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI) supports the
creation and expansion of the processing/preservation capacity through
implementation of various schemes for the overall development of food processing
sector, including creation of modern infrastructure with efficient supply chain
management from farm gate to retail outlet, to reduce post-harvest losses and
create higher value-addition, providing better returns to farmers, creation of
employment opportunities, reducing wastage, increasing the processing level and
enhancing the export of the processed foods. The schemes under MoFPI are as
follows:
i.Central Sector umbrella scheme- Pradhan Mantri Kisan Sampada Yojana
(PMKSY)
ii.Centrally sponsored "PM Formalization of Micro Food Processing Enterprises
(PMFME) Scheme"
27.03.2025 59
iii.Central Sector Production Linked Incentive Scheme for Food Processing Industries
(PLISFPI)
Food irradiation facilitates in reduction of post-harvest losses and wastage
of perishable farm produce by enhancing shelf life. Food irradiation units are
assisted under the Integrated Cold Chain and Value Addition Infrastructure
scheme, one of the component scheme of PMKSY. MoFPI has approved 16
numbers of project proposals for setting up of multi-product food irradiation units
across the Country till date. The total amount of Grants-in-aid of ₹ 112.99 Crore
has been approved of which, ₹ 68.38 Crore has been released.
Further, The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has
formed the Indian Food Sharing Alliance (IFSA) with an aim to address India’s food
waste and hunger crisis by integrating various partner organizations, food recovery
agencies and NGOs. IFSA aims to recover the surplus food generated, reduce the
amount of food waste generated and increase the amount of safe and nutritious
food donated to those in need. FSSAI has framed regulations “Food Safety and
Standards (Recovery and Distribution of Surplus Food) Regulation, 2019” to
promulgate food donation drive and prevent food loss and waste in India.
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) : (cid:174)ी खलीलुर रहमान – उपि(cid:214)थत नह(cid:233) ।

करने का (cid:255)ावधान है।
गजु रात जल संसाधन िवभाग, ि(cid:369)प योजना के अंतग(cid:170)त भाग लेने वाली काया(cid:170)(cid:198)वयन एज(cid:164)िसय(cid:332) म(cid:164)

बांध(cid:332) क(cid:236) 400 करोड़ (cid:321)पये क(cid:236) अनमु ािनत लागत से सुर(cid:177)ा म(cid:164) सधु ार करने संबंधी (cid:255)ावधान मौजूद है।
गजु रात जल संसाधन िवभाग (cid:300)ारा उसके जल भंडारण (cid:177)मता को बढ़ाने/संवध(cid:170)न करने का कोई (cid:255)(cid:214)ताव
नह(cid:233) िकया गया है। सौनी योजना के अंतग(cid:170)त राजकोट शहर म(cid:164) पेयजल आपूित(cid:170) को पूरा करने के िलए
जैसा िक रा(cid:186)य सरकार (cid:300)ारा उ(cid:208)लेख िकया गया है, िक के वल आजी बांध/जलाशय म(cid:164) सं(cid:250)िहत जल का
ही उपयोग िकया जाता है। … ((cid:211)यवधान)

QUALITY MAINTENANCE OF HIGHWAYS
*392. SHRI ANURAG SINGH THAKUR:
SHRI SUNIL KUMAR:
27.03.2025 70
Will the Minister of ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has made provision for five year tenured maintenance
of National Highways (NHs) by the contractors post-construction;
(b) if so, the manner in which the Government would enforce the newly introduced
requirement for contractors to maintain National Highways for up to five years post-
construction;
(c) the mechanisms in place to ensure that maintenance obligations are fulfilled
without contractors cutting costs at the expense of road quality; and
(d) the manner in which the Government would ensure that maintenance records
and quality assessments are transparent and accessible to the public?
THE MINISTER OF ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS (SHRI NITIN JAIRAM
GADKARI):
(a) and (b): The Government has prioritized the maintenance of existing National
Highway (NH) network and inter-alia evolved a mechanism to ensure Maintenance
and Repair (MandR) of all NHs sections through accountable maintenance agency.
NHs projects are executed on mainly three modes i.e. (i) Build Operate and
Transfer (BOT), (ii) Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM) and (iii) Engineering Procurement
and Construction (EPC). Concession period for projects including maintenance on
Build Operate and Transfer (BOT) is 15 to 20 years and on Hybrid Annuity Model
(HAM) is generally 15 years. Concessionaire is responsible for maintenance of the
respective NHs stretches within the concession period of the project. Only in case
of EPC projects, Defect Liability period (DLP) is 5 years for the bituminous
pavement works and 10 years for concrete pavement works.
27.03.2025 71
For Toll-Operate-Transfer (TOT) and InvIT projects, concession period
including maintenance is for 20 to 30 years. Concession period for Projects on
Operate, Maintain and Transfer (OMT) is generally 9 years.
For all remaining sections of NHs stretches where DLP period has ended or
is not under any concession period of BOT/HAM/TOT/InvIT project, Government
has taken a policy decision to undertake maintenance works through Performance
Based Maintenance Contract (PBMC) or Short Term Maintenance Contract
(STMC). While STMC works are generally undertaken for a contract period of 1-2
year, PBMC works are undertaken for a contract period of about 5-7 years. During
the current Financial Year 2024-25, Government has approved STMC works in
17,884 km length costing Rs.2,842 Crore and PBMC works in 6,118 km length
costing Rs.6,757 Crore.
(c): Repair of identified defects/issues in the road condition as well as other
maintenance/repair works are completed by the Contractor/Concessionaire within
the stipulated timeline as per provisions of the Contract. Compliance is ensured
through regular field reports and penalty provisions incorporated in the Contract
documents for action against defaulting Contractor/Concessionaire.
Government encourages the adoption of innovative technologies or methods
to enhance the durability and reduce the maintenance requirements of NHs
(depending upon factors like rainfall, terrain type, soil category etc.). Such
technologies or methods include stabilization of subgrade, geosynthetic reinforced
layer(s) in sub-base/base, concrete roads/whitetopping, perpetual pavement, High
performance bituminous mixes, modified bitumen/bituminous mixes, Fiber
27.03.2025 72
Reinforced Concrete, Cement Grouted Bituminous Mixes, etc. Automated and
Intelligent Machine-Aided Construction has been adopted which ensures cloud
based maintenance records and quality work.
(d): Road condition assessment is carried out through Network Survey Vehicle
(NSV) for the NHs stretches before start of work, before issue of completion
certificate and thereafter at regular intervals of six months after completion of the
work, thereby enabling quality assessment of NHs at regular intervals for (i)
ensuring the maintenance during Concession period / DLP and (ii) prioritizing
maintenance requirements to maintain NHs in traffic worthy condition.
Further, app based monitoring through National Highways Authority of India
(NHAI) One / Tatpar App facilitates, highway project management by Field officers/
Engineers / Contractors / Concessionaires directly from onsite, through digital
reporting of daily and monthly defects, submission of geo-tagged and time-
stamped photos for inspections and digital uploading of test results.
रा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय राजमाग(cid:170)-7 को रा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय राजमाग(cid:170)-39 से जोड़ने वाली सड़क का िनमा(cid:170)ण

(ख) (cid:179)या सरकार क(cid:236) उ(cid:265) माग(cid:170)/सड़क पर अनेक कोयला (cid:202)लॉक और िव(cid:299)तु संय(cid:253)ं मौजूद होने के (cid:320)ि(cid:310)गत

HYDERABAD METRO RAIL PROJECT
*394. SHRI SURESH KUMAR SHETKAR:
Will the Minister of HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the State Government of Telangana has sent Phase-II proposals
for the Hyderabad Metro Rail Projects for two corridors viz. BHEL-Lakdikapul and
27.03.2025 74
Nagole – LB Nagar covering 26 km with estimates of Rs. 9,100 crore and Airport
Metro Corridor from Raidurg to Shamshabad Airport covering 32 km for Rs. 6,250
crore with the request for some modifications to cater to a much larger section of
the commuters and to spur equitable growth in all parts of the city; and
(b) if so, the details thereof and the action taken thereon?
THE MINISTER OF HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS; AND MINISTER OF
POWER (SHRI MANOHAR LAL):
(a) and (b): Government of Telangana has submitted proposal of Hyderabad
Metro Phase-2 project having 5 corridors as per financing option contained in para
E(iii) of the Metro Policy, 2017 extending the existing operational line of Metro
Phase-1 project implemented under PPP model. In the present form, the proposal
needs to undergo intensive appraisal process at different level in Central
Government as per relevant provisions of extant policy and approval of project
depends upon outcome of such appraisal process and availability of resources.
CONSTRUCTION OF HOUSES IN WEST CHAMPARAN UNDER PMAY-U
*395. DR. SANJAY JAISWAL:
Will the Minister of HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS be pleased to state:
(a) the number of houses sanctioned and constructed under the Pradhan Mantri
Awas Yojana – Urban (PMAY-U) in Bihar, particularly in West Champaran during
the last three years and the current year;
(b) the details of the houses completed in the region, district-wise;
27.03.2025 75
(c) whether the Government has reviewed the progress of housing projects in
West Champaran and if so, the outcomes thereof; and
(d) the steps taken/being taken to address the delays and ensure timely
completion of housing projects in the said district?
THE MINISTER OF HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS; AND MINISTER OF
POWER (SHRI MANOHAR LAL):
(a) to (d): ‘Land’ and ‘Colonization’ are State subjects. Therefore, schemes related
to housing for their citizens are implemented by States/Union Territories (UTs).
However, the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) supplements the
efforts of States/UTs by providing Central assistance under Pradhan Mantri Awas
Yojana- Urban (PMAY-U) since 25.06.2015 to provide pucca houses with basic
civic amenities to eligible urban households across the country.
PMAY-U is a demand driven scheme and based on the demand for housing
in the urban areas, States/UTs prepare project proposals and after approval of
State Level Sanctioning and Monitoring Committee (SLSMC), these are submitted
to this Ministry for sanctioning of admissible Central assistance by Central
Sanctioning and Monitoring Committee (CSMC).
Based on the proposals received, a total of 3,14,477 houses have been
sanctioned in the State of Bihar since inception under PMAY-U, out of which
2,92,162 have been grounded for construction and 1,74,892 have been
completed/delivered to the beneficiaries as on 17.03.2025. During the last 3 years
and the current Year (2021-25), a total of 28,705 houses have been sanctioned in
the State of Bihar, 1,00,407 houses have been grounded for construction and
27.03.2025 76
1,03,290 have been completed/delivered to the beneficiaries. District-wise details
of houses sanctioned, grounded and completed/delivered to the beneficiaries since
inception and during the last three years and current year (2021-25) in the State of
Bihar including West Champaran, under PMAY-U are given in the enclosed
Statement.
The timeline for completion of projects varies from State to State and
generally it takes 12-36 months in different verticals of the scheme and as per
Detailed Project Reports (DPRs) of respective projects. The completion timeline for
houses depends on various factors like availability of encumbrance free land,
statutory compliances for start of construction, arrangement of funds by
beneficiaries etc. The Ministry regularly reviews the status of implementation of
PMAY-U with States/UTs through Monthly Progress Reports (MPRs), Monthly
Review Meetings and in the meetings of Central Sanctioning and Monitoring
Committee (CSMC) for completion of sanctioned houses within the extended
scheme period i.e. upto 31.12.2025.
Furthermore, based on the learning from the experiences of 9 years
implementation of PMAY-U, Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs has revamped
the scheme and launched PMAY-U 2.0 ‘Housing for All’ Mission with effect from
01.09.2024 for 1 crore additional eligible beneficiaries at affordable cost through
four verticals i.e., Beneficiary Led Construction (BLC), Affordable Housing in
Partnership (AHP), Affordable Rental Housing (ARH) and Interest Subsidy Scheme
(ISS). The scheme guidelines are available at https://pmay-
urban.gov.in/uploads/guidelines/Operational-Guidelines-of-PMAY-U-2.pdf. As on
27.03.2025 77
date, 32 States/UTs have signed Memorandum of Agreement (MoA) and in
principle approval of more than 6.92 lakh houses has been given to such
States/UTs.

HERITAGE CITY DEVELOPMENT AND AUGMENTATION YOJANA
*397. SHRI KRISHNA PRASAD TENNETI:
Will the Minister of HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS be pleased to state:
(a) the details of list of cities selected under the National Heritage City
Development and Augmentation Yojana (HRIDAY) and the criteria used for their
27.03.2025 80
selection, State-wise and district-wise in Andhra Pradesh including Bapatla
Parliamentary Constituency;
(b) the details and current status of key projects undertaken in these cities for
heritage conservation;
(c) the details of total funds allocated, released and utilized under the said
scheme, State-wise;
(d) the details and the data on the impact of HRIDAY on heritage preservation
and tourism development; and
(e) whether the Government has faced any challenges in the implementation of
HRIDAY and if so, the details thereof along with the action taken to address them?
THE MINISTER OF HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS; AND MINISTER OF
POWER (SHRI MANOHAR LAL):
(a): Heritage City Development and Augmentation Yojana (HRIDAY), a central
sector scheme was launched on 21st January 2015. The main objective of the
scheme is to preserve character and the soul of heritage cities and facilitate
inclusive heritage linked urban development by exploring various avenues
including involving private sector. It was implemented in 12 cities i.e. Ajmer
(Rajasthan), Amaravati (Andhra Pradesh), Amritsar (Punjab), Badami (Karnataka),
Dwarka (Gujarat), Gaya (Bihar), Kancheepuram and Velankanni (Tamil Nadu),
Mathura and Varanasi (Uttar Pradesh), Puri (Odisha), and Warangal (Telangana).
The scheme was initiated as a pilot project consequent to announcement by
the Hon’ble Finance Minister during the Budget Speech of Financial Year 2014-15.
Cities were selected based on their heritage and tourism, religious importance and
27.03.2025 81
geographical spread. The Ministry considered these selection criteria to have a
wide-spread presence of HRIDAY scheme across nation, region, culture and
religion. The heritage development of city is not about beautification and
conservation of just few monuments, but development of entire city in light of its
heritage characteristics, its planning, its cleanliness, sanitation standards, quality
of life to its communities and its economy and livelihoods. Separate efforts are
necessitated under HRIDAY so as to preserve character and the soul of heritage
city which facilitate inclusive heritage linked urban development. To begin with 7
cities viz., Amritsar, Ajmer, Mathura, Gaya, Kancheepuram, Varanasi and
Velankanni were selected to kick-start the scheme for conserving and preserving
their respective heritage characters. Thereafter five more cities i.e. Amaravati,
Badami, Dwarka, Puri, Warangal were included under HRIDAY scheme.
In Andhra Pradesh, Amaravati city is only one of the cities selected under
the HRIDAY Scheme. The details of works undertaken and completed in Amaravati
City of Andhra Pradesh during the mission period are given in the enclosed
Statement-I. No other new cities or projects were taken up in Andhra Pradesh
under the HRIDAY scheme.
(b) and (c): Total outlay for the scheme was Rs. 500 crore. The detailed
current status of key projects undertaken in heritage cities for heritage conservation
and funds allocated, released, and utilized under the scheme State-wise are given
in the enclosed Statement-II and III.
(d): As per the Impact Assessment Study conducted by Quality Council of India
under HRIDAY scheme, various urban development projects were undertaken to
27.03.2025 82
improve infrastructure, promote tourism, and conserve the architectural and
cultural heritage of these cities. In conclusion, HRIDAY scheme across 12 heritage
cities in India highlights the diverse and multifaceted impacts of the scheme. The
HRIDAY scheme has led to notable improvements across various cities, focusing
on heritage conservation, economic growth, and enhanced public infrastructure.
For instance, Cities like Ajmer, Amaravati, and Badami have seen improvements
in cleanliness, tourism, and community development. Amritsar, Gaya, and
Varanasi prioritized heritage revitalization, inclusivity, and economic growth, while
Warangal and Puri witnessed growth in tourism and public amenities.
Kancheepuram and Mathura witnessed improvements in safety and infrastructure,
while Velankanni stood out for its environmental sustainability and heritage
conservation. Overall, the scheme has positively impacted the quality of life for
residents and boosted tourism.
(e): During the implementation of the HRIDAY scheme the role of the concerned
local governments/ULBs was very important for implementing and completing such
projects in a timely manner. In one of the places, the implementation of the HRIDAY
scheme faced lack of coordination in the urban local body that resulted in delay in
completing the project. To address these challenges, the Ministry has conducted
the site visits of the projects and followed up with the Urban Local Bodies for
completion of the project. The mission period of the HRIDAY has ended on 31st
March, 2019. No new cities or projects were taken up after 31st March, 2019 in the
Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs under HRIDAY scheme. On discontinuation
of the HRIDAY scheme, heritage component has been included in National Mission
27.03.2025 83
on Pilgrimage Rejuvenation and Spiritual, Heritage Augmentation Drive
(PRASHAD) which is presently administered by the Ministry of Tourism.

PROCESSING AND SUPPLY CHAIN INFRASTRUCTURE IN FOOD SECTOR
*398. SHRI VIJAYAKUMAR ALIAS VIJAY VASANTH:
Will the Minister of FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES be pleased to state:
(a) the details of the measures implemented to address the low processing
levels in India’s food sector, considering that processing levels average only about
10 percent overall, with fruits at 4.5 percent, vegetables at 2.7 percent, milk at 21.1
percent, meat at 34.2 percent and fishery at 15.4 percent;
(b) whether the Government has any plan to increase the value addition of food
products from 10 percent to 35 percent and processing of fruits and vegetables
27.03.2025 90
from 2 percent to 35 percent by the year 2025, if so, the details thereof along with
specific strategies to achieve these targets;
(c) whether the Government has any target to support this growth and ensure
equitable distribution of benefits across all regions by achieving $ 535 billion by
2025-26 from $ 263 billion in 2019-20 and if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether the Government is aware of the fact that over forty percent of
perishable produce goes to waste due to inadequate processing and supply chain
infrastructure; and
(e) if so, the steps taken/being taken by the Government to reduce food wastage
and improve supply chain efficiencies?
THE MINISTER OF FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES (SHRI CHIRAG
PASWAN):
(a) to (e): The Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI) had conducted a
study through Deloitte titled “Study to Determine the Level of Food Processing in
India”, 2021. As per the study, the level of processing of agriculture products in
India is as follows:
Level of Processing in India (%)
Category/ Commodity Year
2018-19
Fruits 4.5
Vegetables 2.7
Milk 21.1
Meat 34.2
Fish 15.4
27.03.2025 91
For overall development of Food Processing Sector, MoFPI through
implementation of various schemes supports the creation and expansion of the
processing/preservation capacity, including creation of modern infrastructure with
efficient supply chain management from farm gate to retail outlet, to reduce post-
harvest losses and create higher value-addition, providing better returns to farmers,
creation of employment opportunities, reducing wastage, increasing the processing
level and enhancing the export of the processed foods. The schemes under MoFPI
are as follows:
i. Central Sector umbrella scheme- Pradhan Mantri Kisan Sampada Yojana
(PMKSY)
ii.Centrally sponsored "PM Formalization of Micro Food Processing Enterprises
(PMFME) Scheme"
iii.Central Sector Production Linked Incentive Scheme for Food Processing
Industries (PLISFPI)
Under various component schemes of PMKSY, MoFPI mostly provides
financial assistance in the form of grants-in-aid to entrepreneurs for creation of
modern infrastructure and setting up of food processing/preservation industries
including Cold Chains with associated infrastructure like primary processing
facilities, collection centers, pre-conditioning, pre-cooling, ripening, packing etc.
The details of number of projects approved/completed in component schemes of
PMKSY are given as below:

STATUS TO KANNUR INTERNATIONAL AIRPORT
*399. SHRI K. SUDHAKARAN:
Will the Minister of CIVIL AVIATION be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government proposes to extend the 'Point of Call' status to Kannur
International Airport without increasing the existing seat allotment for each carrier
in view of the growing demand for international connectivity and the increasing
passenger traffic in northern Kerala particularly in Kannur;
(b) if so, the details thereof and if not, the reasons therefor;
(c) whether the Government has any alternative suggestions/plans to facilitate the
extension of international carrier services at Kannur Airport and if so, the details
thereof; and
(d) whether the Government would address this long-standing request in the larger
interest of enhancing regional connectivity, promoting economic development and
meeting the travel needs of a significant population in the region considering the
fact that Kannur International Airport plays pivotal role in serving expatriates,
business travelers and the general public in the northern part of Kerala and if so,
the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF CIVIL AVIATION (SHRI KINJARAPU RAMMOHAN NAIDU):
(a) to (d): Grant of point of call (PoC) to the designated carriers of foreign countries
is a continuous process and is done through bilateral agreements on air services
27.03.2025 95
signed between India and foreign countries from time to time. The grant of PoC to
the designated carriers of foreign countries depends on factors which include
benefits to the Indian aviation sector, presence of Indian diaspora in that country,
plans of Indian carriers, elements of reciprocity, balance of benefits and other
relevant factors between two countries.
At present, the Government of India promotes more international operations
by Indian carriers from non-metro points including Kannur either directly or through
their own domestic operations. Accordingly, non-metro points are not granted as
new points of call in the Air Services Agreements (ASAs) to any foreign country.
Moreover, in accordance with the extant guidelines, this Ministry while
allocating traffic rights, gives priority to Indian carriers that intend to operate
scheduled international passenger services from non-metro points.
ROAD ACCIDENTS IN MAHARASHTRA AND BIHAR
*400. SHRI RAVINDRA DATTARAM WAIKAR:
SHRI NARESH GANPAT MHASKE:
Will the Minister of ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) the total number of road accidents reported on National and State highways
in Maharashtra and Bihar during the last five years and the primary causes
identified by the Government thereof;
(b) the specific road safety measures implemented including intelligent traffic
systems, stricter speed regulations and accident-prone zone identification to
reduce fatalities;
27.03.2025 96
(c) whether the Government has initiated any collaborations with private firms
or global road safety experts to adopt best practices and technology-driven
solutions for minimizing road accidents in Maharashtra and Bihar, if so, the details
thereof;
(d) the current status of the proposal to set up emergency response
infrastructure including trauma care centers, highway patrol teams and dedicated
air ambulance services on major highways; and
(e) the steps being taken by the Government to enforce stricter penalties,
improve driving training programmes and regulate overloaded commercial vehicles
which contribute to high number of accidents?
THE MINISTER OF ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS (SHRI NITIN JAIRAM
GADKARI):
(a): Government publishes annual report “Road Accidents in India”, based on
data received from States/UTs. As per reports for the year 2018 to 2022, total
number of road accidents in the State of Maharashtra and Bihar on National
Highways (NHs) and State Highways (SHs) during the calendar year 2018 to 2022
are as under : -
Maharashtra Bihar
Length of NHs Road Accidents Length of NHs (in Road Accidents
(in Km as on 31st on Km as on 31st on
Years March) March)
NHs SHs NHs SHs
NHs SHs NHs SHs
2018 17,765 38,999 9,355 7,755 4,877 4,006 4,016 2,089
2019 17,757 32,005 8,360 7,214 5,358 4,006 4,526 1,945
2020* 17,931 31,992 6,501 5,518 5,358 3,714 4,101 1,711
27.03.2025 97
2021* 18,196 32,486 7,501 6,328 5,771 3,714 4,349 2,115
2022 18,317 NA 9,417 6,902 5,940 3,714 4,601 2,428
* - Covid affected year
As per the data, road accidents occur due to multiple causes such as over
speeding, use of mobile phone, drunken driving/consumption of alcohol and drug,
driving on wrong side/ lane indiscipline, jumping red light, non-use of safety devices
such as helmets and seat belts, vehicular condition, weather condition, road
condition etc.
(b) and (e): The Government in the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways has
formulated a multi-pronged strategy to address the issue of road safety based on
Education, Engineering (both of roads and vehicles), Enforcement and Emergency
Care. Accordingly, various initiatives have been taken by the Government for road
safety in the country as detailed at STATEMENT.
(c): Signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with SaveLIFE Foundation
to collaborate and work towards improving road safety across India. The objective
of the MoU are as under : -
i. to implement Zero-Fatality road corridor programme on the top 100
highest road fatality highways.
ii. to facilitate Zero-Fatality District programme in the top 100 highest
road fatality districts.
iii. to facilitate the complete implementation of the Motor Vehicles
(Amendment) Act, 2019 in the country.
Further, Ministry has approved the proposal of IIT, Madras for setting up of
Centre of Excellence for Road Safety.
27.03.2025 98
(d): Government has taken initiatives to support State Governments towards
setting up of Trauma Care Facilities (TCFs) along the National Highways in the
country under “National Programme for Prevention and Management of Trauma
and Burn Injuries”. During 11th Five Year Plan (2007-2012) and 12th Five Year
Plan (2012-2017), a total of 196 Trauma Care Facilities (TCFs) were sanctioned in
Government Hospitals/ Medical Colleges across the country.
Further, Government has requested all the States/ UTs on 28.12.2022 to set
up dedicated Highway Police Stations at regular intervals along the Highways and
Expressways to ensure efficiency and effectiveness.
STATEMENT
Details of various initiatives taken by the Government in Ministry of Road
Transport and Highways for road safety : -
(1) Education:
i. Administers Road Safety Advocacy Scheme to provide financial assistance to
various agencies for raising awareness about road safety and for administering
road safety programs.
ii. Observance of National Road Safety Month/Week every year for spreading
awareness and strengthening road safety.
iii. Administers a scheme for setting up of Institutes of Driving Training and
Research (IDTRs), Regional Driving Training Centres (RDTCs) and Driving
Training Centres (DTCs) at state/district level across the Country.
(2) Engineering:
2.1. Road engineering:
27.03.2025 99
i. Road Safety Audit (RSA) of all National Highways (NHs) has been made
mandatory through third party auditors/ experts at all stages i.e. design,
construction, operation and maintenance etc.
ii. High priority is accorded to identification and rectification of black spots /accident
spots on NHs.
iii. Road Safety Officer (RSO) has been designated at each Regional Office of road
owning agencies under the Ministry to look after RSA and other road safety related
works.
iv. Administers the electronic Detailed Accident Report (e-DAR) Project to establish
a central repository for reporting, management and analysis of road accidents data
across the Country.
v. Issued guidelines for the provision of signages on Expressways and National
Highways to offer improved visibility and intuitive guidance to the drivers.
vi. Provisions have been made in the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 for failure to comply
with standards for the road design, construction and maintenance, as prescribed
by the Central Government from time to time.
2.2 Vehicle engineering:
Various initiatives were undertaken to make vehicles safer, including the
following:-
i. Mandatory provision of an airbag for the passenger seated on the front seat of a
vehicle, next to the driver.
27.03.2025 100
ii. Prescribed norms related to safety measures for children below four years of
age, riding or being carried on a motor cycle. It also specifies use of a safety
harness, crash helmet and restricts speed to 40kmph.
iii. Mandatory provisions for fitment of following listed safety technologies: -
For M1 category vehicles:
Seat Belt Reminder (SBR) for driver and co-driver.

Manual Override for central locking system

Over speed warning system.

For all M and N category vehicles:
Reverse Parking Alert System

iv. Mandated Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS) for certain classes of L [Motor
vehicle with less than four wheels and includes a Quadricycle], M [Motor vehicles
with at least four wheels used for carrying passengers] and N [Motor vehicles with
at least four wheels used for carrying goods which may also carry persons in
addition to goods, subject to conditions stipulated in BIS standards] categories.
v. Mandated speed limiting function/speed limiting device in all transport vehicles,
except for two wheelers, three wheelers, quadricycles, fire tenders, ambulances
and police vehicles.
vi. Published the rules for recognition, regulation and control of Automated Testing
Stations, which define the procedure for fitness testing of vehicles through
automated equipment and the procedure for grant of fitness certificate by ATSs.
The rules have been further amended on 31.10.2022 and 14.03.2024.
27.03.2025 101
vii. Formulated the Vehicle Scrapping Policy based on incentives/dis-incentives
and for creating an ecosystem to phase out old, unfit and polluting vehicles.
viii. Formulated a Scheme to set up one model Inspection and Certification Centre
in each State/UT with Central assistance for testing the fitness of vehicles through
an automated system.
ix. Published rules regarding the Bharat New Car Assessment Program (BNCAP)
to introduce the concept of safety rating of passenger cars and empower
consumers to take informed decisions.
x. Published rules regarding prescribed level playing field in the area of
manufacturing of buses by Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and Bus
Body Builders.
xi. Mandated vehicles, manufactured on or after 1st October, 2025, shall be fitted
with an air-conditioning system for the cabin of vehicles of N2 (goods vehicle with
gross vehicle weight exceeding 3.5 tonnes but not exceeding 12.0 tonnes) and N3
(goods vehicle with gross vehicle weight exceeding 12.0 tonnes) category.
xii. Published rules for revision of standards for Safety Belt, Restraint Systems and
Safety Belt Reminder to provide provisions for applicability of revised standards for
safety belt assemblies, safety belt anchorages and the installation of Safety Belts
and Restraint Systems, in Motor vehicles of category M,N and L7 w.e.f. 01st April,
2025. Further, the vehicles of category M1, manufactured on and after the 1st April
2025, shall meet the requirement of safety belt reminder for all front facing rear
seats as per AIS-145-2018.
27.03.2025 102
(3) Enforcement:
i. The Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019 as stands implemented provides for
strict penalties for ensuring compliance and enhancing deterrence for violation of
traffic rules and strict enforcement through use of technology. Traffic
management and enforcement is essentially in the domain of State
Governments/UT Administrations. While Central Government formulates rules
under the Motor Vehicle Act, 1988, the enforcement of these rules comes under
the purview of State Governments/UT Administrations. The Government has
specifically requested all States/ UTs for strict implementation of provisions of
Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 related to overloading of vehicles.
ii. Issued rules for Electronic Monitoring and Enforcement of Road Safety. The rules
specify the detailed provisions for placement of electronic enforcement devices on
high risk and high density corridors on National Highways, State Highways and
critical junctions cities having a population of more than one million in the country
and cities under National Clean Air Programme (NCAP).
iii. On 10th June, 2024, the Government has issued an advisory to all the States
and Union territories on technological interventions for ensuring compliance with
Motor Vehicle Act, 1988.
(4) Emergency care:
i. Published rules for the protection of Good Samaritan, who in good faith,
voluntarily and without expectation of any reward or compensation renders
emergency medical or non-medical care or assistance at the scene of an accident
to the victim or transports such victim to the hospital.
27.03.2025 103
ii. Enhanced compensation of victims of Hit and Run motor accidents (from Rs.
12,500 to Rs. 50,000 for grievous hurt and from Rs. 25,000 to Rs.2,00,000 for
death).
iii. The National Highways Authority of India has made provisions for ambulances
with paramedical staff/Emergency Medical Technician/Nurse at toll plazas on the
completed corridors of National Highways.
iv. Government in Ministry of Road Transport and Highways along with National
Health Authority (NHA), has implemented a pilot program for providing cashless
treatment to victims of road accidents in Chandigarh, Haryana, Punjab,
Uttarakhand, Puducherry and Assam.

(ii) Unstarred Question Nos. 4371 to 4600
9TH EDITION OF SWACHH SURVEKSHAN
4371. SHRI YOGENDER CHANDOLIA:
SHRI ALOK SHARMA:
SHRI MAHESH KASHYAP:
SHRI PRAVEEN PATEL:
Will the Minister of HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS be pleased to state:
(a) the number of cities expected to participate in 9th edition of Swachh
Survekshan;
(b) the details of the efforts being made to ensure wide participation across all
urban areas; and
(c) the details of the innovative technologies and methodologies introduced in this
edition to measure cleanliness and sanitation?
27.03.2025 104
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HOUSING AND URBAN
AFFAIRS (SHRI TOKHAN SAHU):
(a) and (b): The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) initiated the
Swachh Survekshan Survey to evaluate and encourage Urban Sanitation
improvements. The inaugural survey was done in 2016 for a sample of cities,
followed by successive editions.
Building on this momentum, under 9th edition of the Swachh Survekshan, all
ULBs notified on or before 31st December, 2023 i.e. 4862 cities/ULBs, are eligible
for assessment. The State-wise details are given in the enclosed Statement.
(c): MoHUA launched the toolkit for Swachh Survekshan, 2024 with the theme
‘Reduce Reuse Recycle’ on 17th January, 2025.ULBs must regularly update their
monthly Management Information System (MIS), which will be validated through
citizen feedback before final rankings are determined by third-party assessors.
After launch of the toolkit, the field assessments have begun on 15thFebruary, 2025.
This edition incorporates new features as detailed under:
1. Separate matrix of indicators for evaluation of cities based on population –
The cities has been divided into5 categories based on their population for ranking.
Very Small Cities Small Cities Medium Cities Big Cities Million plus Cities
< 20,000 20,000 <50,000 50,000 < 3 lakh 3 lakh < 10 10 lakh or more
lakh
2. Simplified indicators in 10 sections–Scoring under Swachh Survekshan,
2024 has been divided in 10 sections viz. Visible Cleanliness, Segregation,
Collection & Transportation of waste, Solid Waste Management, Access to
Sanitation, Used Water Management, Mechanization of desludging services,
27.03.2025 105
Advocacy for Swachhta, Ecosystem Strengthening & Institutional Parameters,
Overall Welfare of Sanitation Workers and Citizen Feedback & Grievance
Redressal.
3. Introduction of “Super Swachh League” - Super Swachh League is
introduced to recognize cities that demonstrate extraordinary performance. Based
on their previous Swachh Survekshan Ranking (SS21, SS22 & SS23) have
become a part of this league for the current year. They will be assessed on
parameters additional to those in the Swachh Survekshan.
4. New indicators for Project grounding, CTUs transformation introduced – to
evaluate the ULB's adherence to mapping all sanctioned projects under SBM and
Cleanliness Target Units (CTUs)identified and improved during the Swachhta Hi
Seva campaign remain free from garbage hotspots and unattended waste,
maintaining a clean and hygienic environment.
5. Special focus on tourists and high footfall places - to ensure cleanliness and
proper maintenance of high footfall areas such as Tourist attractions, Monuments
and parks, Street food zones, Vending zones within the ULB’s jurisdiction.
6. Introduction of school level assessment - to inculcate the habit of maintaining
Swachhata among the students from their younger days to bring in a generational
change in the country.
Swachh Survekshan 2024 Toolkit having detailed bifurcation related to
scoring and validation matrix in respect of all indicators of Swachh Survekshan
2024 is available at: https://sbmurban.org.
27.03.2025 106

SOCIAL SECURITY FOR UNORGANISED ROAD TRANSPORT WORKERS
4372. SHRI ASADUDDIN OWAISI:
Will the Minister of ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware of the lack of social security provisions, such
as Provident Fund (PF) and Employees' State Insurance Corporation (ESI) for
unorganised road transport workers, including drivers and auto rickshaw workers;
and
(b) if so, the steps that are being taken by the Government to extend the said
provisions to ensure their welfare and protection?
THE MINISTER OF ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS (SHRI NITIN JAIRAM
GADKARI):
(a) and (b): Currently the provisions of the Employees' State Insurance Corporation
(ESI) Act, 1948 are not applicable to the unorganised sector which includes
unorganised road transport workers. The Government has formulated the Code on
Social Security, 2020 after amalgamating, simplifying and rationalizing the relevant
provisions of the existing nine Central Acts. The said code inter-alia envisages
extension of social security benefits to unorganised workers, gig workers and
platform workers and the members of their families.
Government has launched eShram portal on 26th August, 2021 for creation
of a comprehensive National Database of Unorganised Workers (NDUW) seeded
with Aadhaar. e-Shram portal is meant to register and support the unorganised
27.03.2025 108
workers by providing them a Universal Account Number (UAN) on a self-declaration
basis. eShram is also integrated with Pradhan Mantri Shram Yogi Maan-dhan (PM-
SYM), which is a pension scheme for unorganised workers who are aged between
18-40 years. It provides a monthly pension of Rs. 3000/- after attaining the age of
60 years. Using UAN, any unorganised worker can easily enrol under PM-SYM. In
the scheme, 50 percent of the contribution is borne by the Government and the rest
is being contributed by the worker. So far, 12 different schemes have already been
integrated/ mapped with the eShram.
SETTING UP OF TRIBAL MONUMENTS AND MUSEUMS
4373. SHRI RAJESHBHAI NARANBHAI CHUDASAMA:
Will the Minister of TRIBAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has set up monuments and museums showcasing
tribes and tribal cultural heritage and their contribution to India's Independence and
nation-building and if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Government proposes to set up such centres at the zonal, divisional
or district level and if so, the details thereof; and
(c) whether programmes are being organized at the local or district level to boost
the self-pride of tribals, most backward and deprived groups and if so, the details
thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF TRIBAL AFFAIRS (SHRI
DURGA DAS UIKEY):
(a): Ministry of Tribal Affairs provides financial assistance under the Centrally
Sponsored Scheme 'Support to Tribal Research Institutes' to States/UTs for setting
27.03.2025 109
up Tribal Freedom Fighters Museum to acknowledge the heroic and patriotic deeds
of tribal people who struggled against the colonial rule and dedicated their life to
the nation. Ministry has approved 11 projects for the construction of museums for
tribal freedom fighters in 10 States. Other than these Ethnographic Museum are
also sanctioned to showcase rich tribal cultural heritage, rare artefacts, dresses,
ornaments, weapons, etc., related to life and culture of various tribes. Under the
scheme, the Ministry provides grants to the State/UT. The State/UT is required to
provide land, prepare detailed project report and implement the project through a
construction and curation agency. Further, as informed, Archaeological Survey of
India, Ministry of Culture is the custodian of built heritage sites, primarily develops
‘Archaeological Site Museums’ to showcase the artefacts recovered from
archaeological explorations, excavations or loose sculptures, detached from
monuments at/near the site, so that the objects do not loose their cultural
significance.
(b): So far, proposal to set up such zonal, divisional or district level centres are not
under consideration.
(c): Ministry of Tribal Affairs, Government of India under the Centrally Sponsored
scheme ‘Support to Tribal Research Institutes (TRIs)’ supports 29 Tribal Research
Institutes (TRIs) in States/Union Territories (UTs) for organization of various
activities which are as under:
i. Tribal festivals, exchange visits by tribals, events like National Tribal Craft
Mela, National/State Tribal Dance Festival, Art Competition, Workshop – cum
– Exhibition on Tribal paintings and state-level tribal poet and writers meet.
27.03.2025 110
ii. Organization of tribal fairs & festivals, festivals like "Medaram Jathara"
undertaken by the Koya Tribe of Telangana, Hornbill Festival of Nagaland,
Sarhul Festival of Jharkhand, Lokotsav of Goa and Pawl Kut festival of
Mizoram, etc.
iii. Moreover, Government of India has declared 15th November as Janjatiya
Gaurav Divas to honour all the tribal freedom fighters to remember and
acknowledge their contribution to the freedom struggle and cultural heritage,
and to re-energize the efforts for the socio-economic development of the tribal
regions. The Ministry of Tribal Affairs along with other Central Ministries, State
Governments, and other Institutions are celebrating the glorious history of its
tribal people, culture, and achievements since 2021. Also, Government of
India is commemorating 150th Birth Anniversary of Bhagwan Birsa Munda as
Janjatiya Gaurav Varsh from 15th November, 2024 to 15th November, 2025.
Further, TRIFED also organizes Adi Mahotsav festival at National level and
State level and Tribal Artisan Melas (TAMs) to identify new artisans and new
products at the sourcing level in States/Districts/Villages for expanding the tribal
producers base.
Moreover, the Ministry of Culture is the Nodal Ministry for the promotion of
culture including tribal culture as part of its larger mandate for protection and
preservation of India’s diverse culture. To protect, promote and preserve various
forms of folk art and culture including tribal culture throughout the country, the
Ministry of Culture has set up seven Zonal Cultural Centres (ZCCs) in the country
27.03.2025 111
during 1985-86 with headquarters at Patiala, Nagpur, Udaipur, Prayagraj, Kolkata,
Dimapur and Thanjavur.

Entrepreneurship Development Programme (EDP) training is provided under
PMEGP to prospective entrepreneurs and beneficiaries whose loan has been
sanctioned by the Banks for setting up PMEGP enterprise. The objective of the
training is to provide orientation and awareness pertaining to various managerial
and operational functions like finance, production, marketing, enterprise

CONSERVATION AND PROMOTION OF TRIBAL CULTURE
4375. SHRI VISHNU DAYAL RAM:
Will the Minister of TRIBAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has undertaken specific initiatives for the preservation,
documentation and promotion of tribal culture and heritage in the country and if so,
the details thereof;
27.03.2025 113
(b) the details of the funds allocated and utilized for the conservation of tribal art,
language and cultural practices during the last three years; and
(c) whether the Government has collaborated with tribal communities, experts or
institutions to ensure the authenticity and sustainability of tribal cultural projects
and if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF TRIBAL AFFAIRS (SHRI
DURGA DAS UIKEY):
(a): Ministry of Tribal Affairs, Government of India has undertaken various initiatives
for the preservation, documentation and promotion of tribal culture and heritage in
the country. Under the Centrally Sponsored scheme ‘Support to Tribal Research
Institutes (TRIs)’ Ministry provides financial support to 29 Tribal Research Institutes
(TRIs) in States/Union Territories (UTs) on the basis of the Annual Action Plan
submitted by the States/UTs subject to the approval of the Apex Committee chaired
by the Secretary, Ministry of Tribal Affairs. Under the Scheme, proposals relating
to infrastructural needs, research & documentation activities and training &
capacity building programmes, organization of tribal festivals, yatras for promotion
of unique cultural heritage and tourism and organisation of exchange visits by
tribals are organised so that their cultural practices, languages and ritual are
preserved and disseminated. Ministry has sanctioned various projects to the Tribal
Research Institutes of the States/UTs towards activities for the socio-cultural,
educational, and economic development of the tribal communities. TRIs are
primarily institutions under the administrative control of the State Government/UT
Administration. The Ministry undertakes following initiatives for the
27.03.2025 114
preservation/documentation and promotion of tribal cultural and heritage which are
as under:
i.Ministry has sanctioned 11 Tribal Freedom Fighters Museums in 10 states to
acknowledge the heroic and patriotic deeds of tribal people and to exhibit rich tribal
cultural heritage of the region. Other than these Ethnographic Museums are also
sanctioned to showcase rare artefacts, dresses, ornaments, weapons etc related
to life and culture of various tribes.
ii.Tribal Research Institutes organise different events like National Tribal Craft Mela,
National/State Tribal Dance Festival, Art Competition, Workshop – cum –
Exhibition on Tribal paintings and state-level tribal poet and writers meet. Further
Ministry provides funds to organize tribal fairs & festivals, festivals like "Medaram
Jathara" undertaken by the Koya Tribe of Telangana, Hornbill Festival of Nagaland,
Sarhul Festival of Jharkhand, Lokotsav of Goa and Pawl Kut festival of Mizoram,
etc.
iii.Research studies/publication of books/documentation including audio visual
documentaries for promotion of rich tribal cultural heritage which includes
preservation of tribal languages.
iv.Research and documentation of Indigenous practices by tribal healers and
medicinal plants, Adivasi Languages, agriculture system, dances and paintings,
organization of literary festivals, publication of books written by tribal writers/
authors, translation works and literature competitions, etc. Preparing Bilingual
Dictionaries, Trilingual Proficiency Modules, Primers for students of Class I, II and
III in tribal languages under Multi-Lingual Education (MLE) Intervention in the line
27.03.2025 115
of New Education Policy 2020. Publishing Varnamala, local rhymes, and stories in
tribal languages. Publishing books, journals on different tribal languages to
promote tribal literature. Documenting folklore, and folktales of different tribes for
preservation and promotion of tribal folk tradition. Collecting oral literature (songs,
riddles, ballads etc.)
v.Ministry has developed a searchable digital repository where all research papers,
books, reports and documents, folks' songs, photos/videos are uploaded. The
repositories can be visited at https://repository.tribal.gov.in/ (Tribal Digital
Document Repository)
vi.Government of India has declared 15th November as Janjatiya Gaurav Divas to
honour all the tribal freedom fighters to remember and acknowledge their
contribution to the freedom struggle and cultural heritage, and to re-energize the
efforts for the socio-economic development of the tribal regions. The Ministry of
Tribal Affairs along with other central ministries, state governments, and other
institutions are celebrating the glorious history of its tribal people, culture, and
achievements since 2021.
vii.Development of bilingual Primers for the preservation of tribal languages and
enhancement of learning achievement levels amongst the Scheduled Tribe
Students. Many language primers have been developed by various State
Governments.
viii.Tribal cultural exchange programmes.
27.03.2025 116
ix.TRIFED also organizes Adi Mahotsav festival at National level and State level and
Tribal Artisan Melas (TAMs) to identify new artisans and new products at the
sourcing level in States/Districts/Villages for expanding the tribal producers base.
Moreover, the Ministry of Culture is the nodal Ministry for the promotion of
culture including tribal culture as part of its larger mandate for protection and
preservation of India’s diverse culture. To protect, promote and preserve various
forms of folk art and culture including tribal culture thourghout the coutry, the
Ministry of Culture has set up seven Zonal Cultural Centres (ZCCs) in the country
during 1985-86 with headquarters at Patiala, Nagpur, Udaipur, Prayagraj, Kolkata,
Dimapur and Thanjavur. Various festivals like Hornbill Festival, Octave, Tribal
Dance Festival, Adi Bimb, Adi Sapta Pallav, Adi Lok Rang, Adiwasi Mahotsav,
Rashtriya Sanskriti Mahotsavs etc. are organised thorugh Zonal Cultural Centres
(ZCCs) of Ministry of Culture to promote & preserve the tribal culture.
Further, Government of India is commemorating 150th Birth Anniversary of
Bhagwan Birsa Munda as Janjatiya Gaurav Varsh from 15th November 2024 to 15th
November 2025.
(b): Under the scheme of Support to TRIs, Ministry has sanctioned various projects/
activities wherein 100% funding is provided by the Central Government on the
proposal submitted by States/UTs. The details of funds released to States/UTs
TRIs under the scheme for the conservation of tribal art, language and cultural
practices during the last three years are given in the enclosed Statement and also
the details of various activities/projects approved under the scheme is available at
the official website of the Ministry https://tribal.nic.in/TRI.aspx
27.03.2025 117
(c): Ministry of Tribal Affairs provides financial assistance under the Central Sector
Scheme “Tribal Research, Information, Education, Communication and Events
(TRI-ECE)”, to reputed research institutions/ organizations/ universities to
undertake various research studies/publication of books/ documentation including
audio visual documentaries to fill the gap of research studies on tribal issues and
promotion of rich tribal cultural, traditions and customs as well as capacity building
of tribal persons/ institutions associated with tribal affairs, dissemination of
information and creation of awareness. Further, as informed, various State/UT
Tribal Research Institute(s) have collaborated with tribal communities, experts or
institutions to ensure the authenticity and sustainability of tribal cultural projects.
